title: loyalty program reference guide
purpose: LLM decision guide — match an onboarding business profile to the right loyalty program type

schema.program_type: name and identifier of the program
schema.best_suited_for: business profile this program fits best
schema.top_industries: industry verticals where this program performs best
schema.customer_engagement: typical engagement level and primary driver
schema.example_brands: real-world brands using this program type

programs.how_it_works: customers earn points per purchase (e.g. 1 pt per $1 spent) and redeem for discounts, free products, or perks
programs.best_suited_for: businesses wanting to reward repeat purchases and track customer behavior over time
programs.top_industries: retail, restaurants, e-commerce, grocery, pharmacies, airlines
programs.key_smb_factors: low cost to launch; scalable; works well with POS systems; points expiry drives urgency

programs.how_it_works: members are grouped into levels (e.g. silver / gold / platinum) with better perks at higher tiers based on spend or activity
programs.best_suited_for: businesses with a range of customer spending levels who want to reward top customers and motivate mid-tier customers to spend more
programs.top_industries: airlines, hotels, beauty, fashion, credit cards, luxury goods
programs.key_smb_factors: requires clear tier logic; best when top customers are significantly more valuable; can feel out of reach for casual buyers
programs.example_brands: Marriott Bonvoy, Sephora Beauty Insider, American Airlines AAdvantage

programs.how_it_works: customers earn a % of each purchase back as store credit or cash (e.g. 2% back on every $1 spent)
programs.best_suited_for: businesses where customers are price-sensitive and prefer immediate, tangible value over complex reward systems
programs.top_industries: credit cards, grocery, fuel, e-commerce, general retail, financial services
programs.key_smb_factors: very easy to explain; high opt-in rate; margin impact needs careful planning; best when average order value is high
programs.example_brands: Rakuten, Chase Freedom, Costco, Target Circle

programs.how_it_works: customers collect a stamp or punch per visit/purchase; after a set number they earn a free item or discount (e.g. buy 9 coffees, get 1 free)
programs.best_suited_for: local, high-frequency businesses with a simple, single-product or limited-menu offering
programs.top_industries: cafés, bakeries, hair salons, car washes, dry cleaners, food trucks
programs.key_smb_factors: near-zero cost to implement; no software needed; easy for staff; limited data capture; digital versions (apps) extend the model
programs.example_brands: local coffee shops, Subway (legacy), nail salons

programs.how_it_works: all members earn Stars per dollar spent. tier determines earn rate: Green = 1 Star/$1 · Gold (500 Stars/yr) = 1.2 Stars/$1 · Reserve (2,500 Stars/yr) = 1.7 Stars/$1. higher tiers unlock Stars that never expire, more Double Star Days, extended birthday reward windows, and exclusive menu access.
programs.best_suited_for: high-frequency brands with a wide customer spectrum — from casual occasional buyers to daily loyalists; especially effective when top customers visit 5–10x more than the average customer
programs.top_industries: coffee, fast casual restaurants, retail, beauty, fitness, any brand with daily or weekly visit cadence
programs.key_smb_factors: run flat points first. once data shows a top-spending cohort (~15–20% of customers driving 60%+ of revenue), add a 2nd tier with faster earn rate and one exclusive perk. Starbucks' original Green/Gold two-tier model is a practical SMB template.
programs.example_brands: Starbucks Rewards (34.6M US members, Q1 FY25) — applicable as a template for any high-frequency SMB with a loyalty app or POS integration
